preFix.title: Cockerel management and flock dynamics
preFix.subtitle: When a cockerel helps, when he hinders, and how to manage the flock around him.
preFix.excerpt: A cockerel is not required for egg production, but he changes the flock in ways a keeper needs to understand before acquiring one. This tutorial covers the practical questions: hen-to-cockerel ratio, feather damage, handling an aggressive male, and what happens when two cockerels share a space.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, 2018. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. University of Wisconsin Extension, Backyard Poultry Series.

content.text: is needed only if you want fertile eggs. His presence in a flock changes the social structure, the feather condition of the hens, and the noise level. Whether he is worth the trade-offs depends on what the flock is for.

A good cockerel calls the flock to food, positions himself at the flock's edge when the hens forage, and breaks up squabbles between hens. In a free-range flock with enough space, he has a legitimate functional role. He is not, however, essential for the hens to lay, settle into a hierarchy, or live contentedly.

The problems a cockerel introduces are proportion-dependent. With too few hens relative to his activity level, he will over-mate the hens he prefers, causing bald patches on backs and necks from repeated treading.
The general guidance is a minimum of 8-10 hens per cockerel. In a small garden flock of 4-6 hens, one cockerel is almost always too many; 12+ hens gives him enough to spread his attention. The

also depends on breed. Heavy breeds mate less frequently than light Mediterranean types; bantam cockerels are less persistent than their large counterparts. If feather damage is appearing on the hens, the ratio is wrong.

show bald patches on the back of the head, the back of the neck, and the saddle area above the tail. Hen saddles (small fabric aprons) protect the feathers without removing the cockerel. Alternatively, move the worst-affected hen into a separate run for two to three weeks to let her recover feathers, then reintroduce.

Two unrelated adult males in the same flock will fight until one establishes dominance, and then the subordinate male will be harassed continuously. Two cockerels in the same space rarely works unless the space is very large, the flock is very big (30+ hens), and there are clear visual barriers between feeding and roosting areas. With a standard smallholding flock, keep one.

A cockerel that attacks the keeper should be caught and carried under the arm, breast facing outward, for ten minutes each day until the behaviour stops. This signals dominance without injury to the bird. A cockerel that attacks children or charges repeatedly despite handling should be culled; aggression toward humans is heritable and the behaviour does not reliably improve.

Most cockerels are not aggressive. Breeds selected for docility (Orpington, Sussex, Plymouth Rock) rarely cause problems. Aggressive strains tend to come from competitive game-breed lines or from cockerels that were not handled regularly as young birds.
